title: Multiple origins of the cortical gamma rhythm
rationale: Review that maps distinct gamma generators (PING vs persistent/ING) onto distinct computational roles, arguing the E-I timing signature determines what functional operation (input selection vs local processing) the rhythm supports.

title: Coherent oscillations: A mechanism of feature linking in the visual cortex?
rationale: Proposes coherent oscillations as a feature-linking substrate but treats the oscillation as given; no explicit PING/E-I generator is invoked as the vehicle.

title: Synchronous oscillations in neuronal systems: Mechanisms and functions
rationale: Review that explicitly links inhibitory-interneuron / E-I network mechanisms generating gamma synchrony to the feature-binding and assembly-formation functions they are proposed to serve.

title: A model for the neuronal implementation of selective visual attention based on temporal correlation among neurons
rationale: Selective attention arises because synchronized (gamma) firing of attended neurons is preferentially transmitted downstream, making network-generated temporal correlation the vehicle for the attentional-selection function.

title: Gamma oscillations as a mechanism for selective information transmission
rationale: Follow-up analysis claiming gamma-synchronised input selectively drives a downstream neuron (coincidence-detection / CTC-style selection); the oscillatory E-I input timing is the named mechanism for selective transmission.

title: Attentional modulation of firing rate and synchrony in a model cortical network
rationale: A PING cortical model where attention increases both gamma synchrony and firing rate of the attended assembly, deriving attentional gain from the E-I rhythm.

title: Biased competition through variations in amplitude of γ-oscillations
rationale: Shows the amplitude of E-I gamma sets which of two competing pools wins downstream read-out — biased competition follows directly from the pyramidal-interneuron gamma mechanism.

title: An oscillation-based model for the neuronal basis of attention
rationale: Argues selective attention is implemented by modulating gamma-range synchrony among competing neuronal populations, with inhibitory interaction shaping the oscillation that carries the attentional tag.

title: Sub-millisecond coincidence detection in active dendritic trees
rationale: Develops single-neuron coincidence detection as a computational function, but no PING/E-I gamma mechanism is named as the source of the coincident inputs.

title: Temporal structure in spatially organized neuronal ensembles: a role for interneuronal networks
rationale: Explicitly proposes GABAergic interneuronal networks as the generator that imposes fast-oscillatory temporal structure on ensembles, and argues this temporal framing is functional for information coding and read-out.

title: Prefrontal Parvalbumin Neurons in Control of Attention
rationale: PFC PV-interneuron (gamma) activity causally controls attention

title: The role of neuronal synchronization in selective attention
rationale: Review of attention-related gamma synchronization that is largely correlational; it does not commit the gamma to a specified PING/E-I generating mechanism as the vehicle for the function.

title: Communication through coherence with inter-areal delays
rationale: Refines CTC by incorporating conduction delays and explicitly invokes PING as the gamma generator whose phase gates inter-areal communication, a direct mechanism->function paper.

title: Phase-of-Firing Coding of Natural Visual Stimuli in Primary Visual Cortex
rationale: Famous phase-of-firing coding relative to low-frequency LFP with no generating mechanism named — phenomenology, PING/E-I is not the invoked vehicle.

title: Distributed Attention Is Implemented through Theta-Rhythmic Gamma Modulation
rationale: theta-gamma sampling, PING origin not the vehicle (OUT)

title: Inhibitory synchrony as a mechanism for attentional gain modulation
rationale: Argues that the degree of interneuron (inhibition-based) gamma synchrony sets the temporal window for coincidence detection and thereby the attentional gain of a cortical cell.

title: Synchronization as a mechanism for attentional gain modulation
rationale: Conference companion to the J Physiol Paris paper: inhibitory (E-I gamma) synchrony narrows the coincidence window so attentional changes in synchrony modulate output gain — mechanism is the named vehicle.

title: Stimulus competition by inhibitory interference
rationale: Proposes that stimulus competition is resolved by interference between the gamma-timed inhibitory (interneuron) rhythms driven by each stimulus, making E-I/inhibitory gamma timing the explicit mechanism of competition (ING-flavoured but the competing inhibitory loop is the named vehicle).

title: Selective attention through phase relationship of excitatory and inhibitory input synchrony in a model cortical neuron
rationale: Argues the phase relationship between synchronous excitatory and inhibitory (E-I gamma) inputs gates whether a neuron responds, providing a phase-based mechanism for selective attention — the E-I synchrony loop is the vehicle.

title: Inhibitory Postsynaptic Potentials Carry Synchronized Frequency Information in Active Cortical Networks
rationale: Shows synchronized interneuron IPSPs (the inhibitory arm of the E-I gamma loop) time pyramidal spikes and carry gamma-frequency information — mechanism as vehicle for temporal coding.

title: Shunting Inhibition Improves Robustness of Gamma Oscillations in Hippocampal Interneuron Networks by Homogenizing Firing Rates
rationale: Mechanistic result on shunting inhibition and oscillation robustness; robustness is a network property, not a cognitive/computational function.

title: Recruitment of Parvalbumin-Positive Interneurons Determines Hippocampal Function and Associated Behavior
rationale: Genetically altering PV-PV interneuron coupling changes gamma synchrony and produces behavioral/cognitive deficits — the interneuron-network gamma mechanism is tied to a functional/behavioral outcome.

title: Spike-Phase Coding Boosts and Stabilizes Information Carried by Spatial and Temporal Spike Patterns
rationale: Strong phase-of-firing result but the reference phase is a slow (delta/theta) LFP and no generating mechanism is named — pure phenomenology with respect to PING/E-I, and not gamma-generator-based.

title: Cortical Enlightenment: Are Attentional Gamma Oscillations Driven by ING or PING?
rationale: Review that explicitly frames attentional gamma as generated by either ING or PING and argues which E-I loop supports attentional gain/routing — mechanism-to-function is the whole thesis.

title: Oscillations and Filtering Networks Support Flexible Routing of Information
rationale: E-I oscillatory filtering networks are the mechanism by which oscillation-tagged signals are selectively routed/demultiplexed, an explicit gating-from-mechanism argument.

title: Differences in Gamma Frequencies across Visual Cortex Restrict Their Possible Use in Computation
rationale: Skeptical empirical argument that stimulus-dependent gamma frequency variation undermines binding-by-synchrony; it is phenomenology and does not name a PING/E-I generator as the functional vehicle.

title: Attentional Stimulus Selection through Selective Synchronization between Monkey Visual Areas
rationale: OUT (strict CTC fork): empirical CTC support for attentional selection via gamma coherence, but the gamma generator (PING/E-I) is not named as the mechanistic vehicle.

title: Rates and Rhythms: A Synergistic View of Frequency and Temporal Coding in Neuronal Networks
rationale: Review arguing that inhibition-based gamma (PING/ING) provides the temporal reference frame for spike-timing/temporal coding on top of rate coding — mechanism-to-coding-function throughout.

title: Canonical Microcircuits for Predictive Coding
rationale: superficial-layer E-I gamma as vehicle for feedforward prediction-error

title: Robust Gamma Coherence between Macaque V1 and V2 by Dynamic Frequency Matching
rationale: Argues PING-generated gamma achieves robust inter-areal coherence via dynamic frequency matching, tying the E-I generator mechanism to the CTC communication function.

title: The Theta-Gamma Neural Code
rationale: Canonical phase-coding claim (items held in successive gamma slots within theta), but the gamma is treated as a generic inhibitory slot/gating mechanism rather than a named reciprocal pyramidal-interneuron PING loop, so the generating mechanism is not explicitly PING/E-I.

title: Attentional Modulation of Cell-Class-Specific Gamma-Band Synchronization in Awake Monkey Area V4
rationale: OUT: cell-class-specific (interneuron vs pyramidal) gamma is consistent with an E-I loop but the paper is empirical correlation with attention, not a mechanism-as-vehicle argument.

title: Visual Areas Exert Feedforward and Feedback Influences through Distinct Frequency Channels
rationale: Frames gamma from the canonical microcircuit E-I loop as the feedforward channel (vs alpha-beta feedback), so the PING rhythm is the vehicle for directed inter-areal routing in the visual hierarchy.

title: Gamma Rhythms Link Prefrontal Interneuron Dysfunction with Cognitive Inflexibility in Dlx5/6+/− Mice
rationale: PV dysfunction degrades PFC gamma; restoring it rescues rule-shift

title: Rhythms for Cognition: Communication through Coherence
rationale: CTC 2.0 explicitly names PING as the gamma generator and argues flexible routing/gating of information between groups follows from the phase of that pyramidal-interneuron rhythm.

title: Gamma and Beta Bursts Underlie Working Memory
rationale: Out: links gamma bursts to working-memory encoding but treats bursts phenomenologically without naming the PING/E-I loop as the generating mechanism that produces the function.

title: Gamma-Rhythmic Gain Modulation
rationale: Shows locally generated gamma imposes a rhythmic gain on responses so that input gain depends on gamma phase (coincidence/gain axis) — the E-I gamma rhythm is the vehicle for phase-dependent gain.

title: Laminar Organization of Attentional Modulation in Macaque Visual Area V4
rationale: Out: characterizes layer-resolved attentional modulation of gamma but does not argue a cognitive function follows from a named PING/E-I generating mechanism.

title: Distinct Inhibitory Circuits Orchestrate Cortical beta and gamma Band Oscillations
rationale: mechanism/temporal-scale claim, no downstream function (OUT)

title: Gamma Synchronization between V1 and V4 Improves Behavioral Performance
rationale: Out under the strict-CTC fork: it empirically links V1-V4 gamma coherence to faster behavior but does not name the PING/E-I loop as the generator vehicle, so it is phenomenology of coherence-to-behavior.

title: Persistent Gamma Spiking in SI Nonsensory Fast Spiking Cells Predicts Perceptual Success
rationale: Fast-spiking interneuron gamma spiking (the inhibitory arm of the PING loop) predicts perceptual detection, arguing the E-I gamma mechanism gates sensory-evoked perception.

title: A Distinct Class of Bursting Neurons with Strong Gamma Synchronization and Stimulus Selectivity in Monkey V1
rationale: Identifies a bursting cell class whose strong PING gamma synchronization co-occurs with sharp stimulus selectivity, arguing gamma-synchronized bursts are the vehicle for selective feedforward signaling.

title: A mechanism for inter-areal coherence through communication based on connectivity and oscillatory power
rationale: Skeptical reversal of CTC: argues E-I-generated gamma coherence is a consequence of feedforward communication and pooled power rather than its cause, directly contesting the PING-coherence-drives-routing claim.

title: Cortical VIP neurons locally control the gain but globally control the coherence of gamma band rhythms
rationale: Dissociates cell-type roles in the gamma E-I loop, arguing VIP disinhibition sets local gain while shaping the coherence of PING gamma that supports inter-areal communication.

title: A mechanism for cognitive dynamics: neuronal communication through neuronal coherence
rationale: Foundational CTC paper: rhythmic inhibition from the gamma-generating interneuron network opens/closes excitability windows, and effective inter-group communication follows FROM the phase relation of that E-I rhythm.

title: Gamma oscillations: precise temporal coordination without a metronome
rationale: Argues the self-organized E-I interneuron gamma network provides precise temporal coordination of spikes (a code) without an external clock — coordination function tied to the recurrent inhibitory mechanism.

title: Do gamma oscillations play a role in cerebral cortex?
rationale: Skeptical review arguing that stimulus-dependent variability of the E-I gamma rhythm undermines proposed coding/CTC functions, engaging (and rejecting) the mechanism-to-function argument.

title: Interneuron Diversity series: Inhibitory interneurons and network oscillations in vitro
rationale: Review explicitly deriving temporal-coding/binding function from interneuron-based (ING/PING) gamma: the inhibitory network sets the synchronizing time base for principal-cell spike timing.

title: Interneuron Diversity series: Fast in, fast out – temporal and spatial signal processing in hippocampal interneurons
rationale: Argues that submillisecond fast excitation-inhibition signaling in interneurons provides the narrow temporal window enabling coincidence detection and gamma-timescale synchronization — mechanism tied to a temporal-processing function, though function is secondary.

title: The gamma cycle
rationale: Argues the gamma cycle is an excitation-then-inhibition duty cycle produced by the E-I (interneuron) loop, and that this rhythmic inhibitory window IS the vehicle for phase coding, coincidence detection, and gain modulation of downstream reading.

title: Metastability and Coherence: Extending the Communication through Coherence Hypothesis Using a Whole-Brain Computational Perspective
rationale: Out: whole-brain metastable coherence extension of CTC at the macroscopic level; the coherence is not tied to a named PING/E-I gamma generator, so it is mechanism-agnostic for this scope.

title: Inhibitory Interneurons Regulate Temporal Precision and Correlations in Cortical Circuits
rationale: review: FS-interneuron rhythmic inhibition sets temporal precision

title: Neuronal networks for induced ‘40 Hz’ rhythms
rationale: Review arguing the interneuron-network (ING/E-I) mechanism for induced 40 Hz rhythms provides the synchronization substrate for feature binding, explicitly linking generator mechanism to cognitive function.

title: Integrator or coincidence detector? The role of the cortical neuron revisited
rationale: Argues that in the gamma-synchronous regime generated by cortical E-I interaction, neurons operate as coincidence detectors with a narrow temporal window — the coincidence/gain functional axis explicitly following from the synchrony mechanism (year is 1996, at edge of pre-1996 index).

title: Inhibition-based rhythms: experimental and mathematical observations on network dynamics
rationale: Foundational review of ING vs PING that ties the E-I timing mechanisms to functional roles (synchronization for binding and cross-area communication); makes the mechanism->function argument explicitly.

title: Submillisecond AMPA Receptor-Mediated Signaling at a Principal Neuron–Interneuron Synapse
rationale: Characterizes the fast excitatory drive onto interneurons (the E→I arm of PING) but makes no functional claim; pure synaptic mechanism.

title: The Temporal Correlation Hypothesis of Visual Feature Integration
rationale: OUT: defends binding-by-synchrony, but as a synchrony-code hypothesis; no E-I gamma-generating mechanism is the named vehicle.

title: Neuronal Synchrony: A Versatile Code for the Definition of Relations?
rationale: OUT: argues synchrony codes relations (binding), but the generative E-I/PING mechanism is not the vehicle of the claim.

title: Synchrony Unbound
rationale: Foundational skeptical critique of binding-by-synchrony; targets the coding claim generally and does not invoke PING/E-I as the gamma generator, so it fails the mechanism-as-vehicle criterion despite being the key critical piece.

title: The What and Why of Binding
rationale: Correlation/temporal-binding theory of feature grouping, but framed abstractly with no PING/E-I gamma mechanism as the generator.

title: Optimal information transfer in synchronized neocortical neurons
rationale: Argues that inhibitory gamma synchrony tunes neurons to a regime of optimal (maximal) information transfer via reliable spike timing — synchronizing inhibition is the named vehicle for the information-transfer function.

title: Simulation of Gamma Rhythms in Networks of Interneurons and Pyramidal Cells
rationale: Classic PING simulation establishing how interneuron+pyramidal networks generate gamma, but it is a mechanism/modeling paper with no functional claim derived from it.

title: New Roles for the Gamma Rhythm: Population Tuning and Preprocessing for the Beta Rhythm
rationale: Argues PING gamma performs population tuning/normalization (a computational preprocessing function) selecting the most-excited cells before handing off to beta — computation follows from the E-I loop.

title: Cholinergic induction of network oscillations at 40 Hz in the hippocampus in vitro
rationale: Demonstrates cholinergically-induced PING-type gamma with E-I phase relationships, but is mechanism/phenomenology and does not argue a downstream cognitive function.

title: Oscillatory responses in cat visual cortex exhibit inter-columnar synchronization which reflects global stimulus properties
rationale: OUT: foundational binding-by-synchrony phenomenology — synchrony codes global stimulus properties, but no E-I/PING generator is named as the mechanism.

title: Correlated neuronal activity and the flow of neural information
rationale: OUT: argues correlated/synchronous input gates information flow via coincidence detection, but treats synchrony/correlation generically and does not name PING (or a specific E-I gamma loop) as the generator — no PING-mechanism-to-function link.

title: Dynamic predictions: Oscillations and synchrony in top–down processing
rationale: OUT: broad review of synchrony in top-down processing; function attributed to synchrony per se, not to a named E-I gamma-generating mechanism.

title: Synchronized oscillations in interneuron networks driven by metabotropic glutamate receptor activation
rationale: Foundational ING paper demonstrating mutual-inhibition gamma generation, but it is pure mechanism and does not itself argue a cognitive/computational function follows.

title: Pattern recognition computation using action potential timing for stimulus representation
rationale: Proposes phase-of-oscillation spike-timing codes for stimulus representation (phase-coding function), but the reference oscillation is an abstract subthreshold clock, not a PING/E-I gamma generator.

title: A mechanism for generation of long-range synchronous fast oscillations in the cortex
rationale: Classic mechanism for long-range gamma synchrony motivated by binding-by-synchrony, but the paper is about how the synchrony arises, not a developed functional claim — judged mechanism, binding only as motivation.

title: Visuomotor integration is associated with zero time-lag synchronization among cortical areas
rationale: Empirical evidence linking zero-lag gamma synchrony to visuomotor binding; pure phenomenology with no E-I generating mechanism named.

title: A critical role for NMDA receptors in parvalbumin interneurons for gamma rhythm induction and behavior
rationale: PV-NMDAR deletion disrupts PV-gamma and produces behavioral deficits

title: Inhibitory feedback required for network oscillatory responses to communication but not prey stimuli
rationale: Reciprocal pyramidal-interneuron (E-I) feedback generates a gamma-band network oscillation that is functionally required specifically for encoding communication (global) stimuli, making the E-I feedback-loop rhythm the vehicle for stimulus-class selection.

title: Gamma-band synchronization in visual cortex predicts speed of change detection
rationale: OUT: famous gamma-behavior correlation (gamma synchronization predicts reaction time) with no generating E-I mechanism named as the vehicle — pure phenomenology.

title: Parvalbumin neurons and gamma rhythms enhance cortical circuit performance
rationale: Demonstrates PV-interneuron-generated gamma (the E-I mechanism) enhances signal transmission and reduces circuit noise — improved cortical performance attributed to that gamma-generating loop.

title: Driving fast-spiking cells induces gamma rhythm and controls sensory responses
rationale: Optogenetically shows fast-spiking (PV) interneurons — the I in the E-I loop — generate gamma, and that gamma phase controls the gain and timing of sensory responses, i.e. function follows from the interneuron mechanism.

title: Dynamic information routing in complex networks
rationale: Out: framed as generic collective-oscillation phase-switching routing in oscillator networks; PING/E-I is not named as the specific gamma generator, so it is mechanism-agnostic routing theory.

title: Dynamic circuit motifs underlying rhythmic gain control, gating and integration
rationale: Review casting E-I interneuron circuit motifs (the PING loop) as the generators of gamma whose timing performs rhythmic gain control, gating and integration — an explicit mechanism->function argument.

title: Gamma-range synchronization of fast-spiking interneurons can enhance detection of tactile stimuli
rationale: FS-interneuron gamma synchronization phase causally enhances tactile detection

title: Cortical gamma band synchronization through somatostatin interneurons
rationale: Shows SOM interneurons form the inhibitory arm of a spatially extended PING loop, and that this cell-type-specific E-I gamma is the vehicle for long-range synchronization/surround integration.

title: Flexible information routing by transient synchrony
rationale: Transient collective gamma oscillations arising from E-I interaction route information along stimulus-dependent paths, making the transient PING-type synchrony the vehicle for flexible routing.

title: Targeted optogenetic stimulation and recording of neurons in vivo using cell-type-specific expression of Channelrhodopsin-2
rationale: methods/protocol companion, no functional claim (OUT)

title: Synaptic mechanisms of synchronized gamma oscillations in inhibitory interneuron networks
rationale: Canonical Bartos-Jonas ING mechanism review; overwhelmingly about how gamma is generated, with only brief gesturing at function — no sustained mechanism-to-cognition argument.

title: Regulation of spike timing in visual cortical circuits
rationale: Nat Rev Neurosci review arguing that inhibition-based gamma synchrony (PING/ING E-I loops) regulates precise spike timing to implement gain control, coincidence detection and attentional selection — an explicit mechanism-to-function synthesis.

title: Oscillatory multiplexing of population codes for selective communication in the mammalian brain
rationale: Review arguing E-I gamma oscillations multiplex and selectively route population codes, connecting the inhibition-based rhythm mechanism to the communication function.

title: Cross-hemispheric gamma synchrony between prefrontal parvalbumin interneurons supports behavioral adaptation during rule shift learning
rationale: interhemispheric PV gamma synchrony supports rule-shift adaptation

title: A role for fast rhythmic bursting neurons in cortical gamma oscillations in vitro
rationale: Identifies FRB (chattering) neurons as drivers of cortical gamma; a cellular-mechanism paper without a derived cognitive/computational function.

title: Background gamma rhythmicity and attention in cortical local circuits: A computational study
rationale: Argues that a background PING gamma rhythm lets a weak attentional bias selectively boost a target assembly's coherence while suppressing distractors — attention/stimulus selection follows directly from the E-I gamma mechanism.

title: A beta2-frequency (20–30 Hz) oscillation in nonsynaptic networks of somatosensory cortex
rationale: Out of scope: the rhythm is generated by gap-junction / nonsynaptic mechanisms, not the reciprocal E-I (PING/ING) loop, so it fails the mechanism gate.

title: On the formation of gamma-coherent cell assemblies by oriens lacunosum-moleculare interneurons in the hippocampus
rationale: Explains how OLM interneurons shape theta-nested gamma cell assemblies mechanistically, but frames it as network dynamics without an explicit cognitive/computational claim — borderline, judged pure mechanism.

title: Gamma oscillations mediate stimulus competition and attentional selection in a cortical network model
rationale: PING-generated gamma is the explicit vehicle by which competing stimulus assemblies inhibit each other so that the more strongly driven one wins — biased competition and attentional selection from the E-I loop.

title: Hippocampal theta rhythm and its coupling with gamma oscillations require fast inhibition onto parvalbumin-positive interneurons
rationale: Shows fast inhibition onto PV cells is required for theta-gamma coupling; focuses on rhythm-generation mechanism rather than a stated cognitive function.

title: Laminar differences in gamma and alpha coherence in the ventral stream
rationale: descriptive laminar profile, no mechanism-function argument (OUT)

title: Cortical oscillations arise from contextual interactions that regulate sparse coding
rationale: In an inhibition-stabilized E-I network, gamma oscillations emerge from contextual interactions and regulate response gain/sparse coding, making the E-I mechanism the vehicle for gain-control function.

title: Fast synaptic inhibition promotes synchronized gamma oscillations in hippocampal interneuron networks
rationale: Pure ING generation mechanism (fast/strong GABAergic synapses synchronize interneurons); no functional/cognitive claim made.

title: Stimulus-specific neuronal oscillations in orientation columns of cat visual cortex.
rationale: OUT: reports stimulus-specific gamma oscillations (pure phenomenology); no functional claim derived from a named E-I generating mechanism.

title: Synaptic background activity influences spatiotemporal integration in single pyramidal cells.
rationale: Shows background synaptic input controls the integration window and gain of pyramidal cells (gain axis), but is about tonic background conductance, not a PING/E-I gamma rhythm as the vehicle.

title: Coherent 40-Hz oscillation characterizes dream state in humans.
rationale: Human MEG 40 Hz phenomenology tied to conscious/dream states, but the proposed generator is thalamocortical resonance, not a cortical PING/E-I gamma loop.

title: Synchronization of oscillatory responses in visual cortex correlates with perception in interocular rivalry
rationale: OUT: synchrony correlates with perceptual selection in rivalry (phenomenology); no PING/E-I generator invoked.

title: Gamma rhythms and beta rhythms have different synchronization properties
rationale: Shows the E-I loop that generates gamma cannot synchronize over long conduction delays whereas beta can, arguing gamma is for local and beta for long-range communication/binding — a routing/communication function tied to the mechanism.

title: Information transfer in entrained cortical neurons
rationale: Argues that entrainment of cortical neurons by rhythmic inhibitory (gamma) drive regulates spike-timing reliability and thereby the amount of information transferred — inhibition-based gamma entrainment is the vehicle for the information-transfer claim.

title: Robust gamma oscillations in networks of inhibitory hippocampal interneurons
rationale: OUT: pure mechanism paper on how inhibitory (ING) interneuron networks robustly generate gamma against heterogeneity/noise; no cognitive/computational function is claimed to follow from the mechanism.

title: Cell Type-Specific Control of Spike Timing by Gamma-Band Oscillatory Inhibition
rationale: Argues that gamma-band inhibition from the E-I loop controls the spike timing of specific cell types, making the PING rhythm the vehicle for temporal coding/precision.

title: Memory retrieval time and memory capacity of the CA3 network: Role of gamma frequency oscillations
rationale: Interneuron feedback gamma provides the periodic inhibitory window that clocks and bounds autoassociative retrieval, the E-I loop determining which cell assembly reaches threshold each cycle.

title: Some Reflections on Visual Awareness
rationale: Proposes ~40 Hz synchronization as the substrate for binding and awareness, but treats the oscillation as given — no E-I/PING generator is named as the vehicle.

title: Temporal Decorrelation of Collective Oscillations in Neural Networks with Local Inhibition and Long-Range Excitation
rationale: OUT: characterises the dynamics/decorrelation of E-I gamma oscillations as a mechanism but makes no explicit cognitive/computational function claim (attention, selection, routing) that follows from it — pure mechanism/dynamics.

title: Neurosystems: brain rhythms and cognitive processing
rationale: Review that maps specific E-I rhythm mechanisms (including PING gamma) onto cognitive functions such as attention, routing and gating — the mechanism->function mapping is its purpose.

title: Attention – oscillations and neuropharmacology
rationale: Review linking the E-I (interneuron/cholinergic) generation of gamma to attentional gain and selection, an explicit mechanism->attention argument.

title: Cholinergic control of cortical network interactions enables feedback-mediated attentional modulation
rationale: E-I spiking model in which cholinergic tuning of the gamma-generating loop enables feedback to bias the competition — the E-I gamma interaction is the named vehicle for attentional modulation.

title: Analysis of gamma rhythms in the rat hippocampus in vitro and in vivo.
rationale: Characterizes and models the E-I gamma mechanism and its spatial synchrony but makes no explicit cognitive-function argument; mechanism only.

title: Feedforward and feedback frequency-dependent interactions in a large-scale laminar network of the primate cortex
rationale: Laminar E-I model in which superficial-layer gamma carries feedforward and deep-layer alpha-beta carries feedback, making the PING gamma rhythm the vehicle for frequency-multiplexed inter-areal routing.

title: Modulation of Oscillatory Neuronal Synchronization by Selective Visual Attention
rationale: OUT: landmark empirical link between attention and gamma synchrony, but a correlation — no PING/E-I generator invoked as the mechanistic vehicle.

title: Neuronal Oscillations in Cortical Networks
rationale: Broad review of many oscillatory bands and mechanisms; it does not center a single PING-gamma->function argument, so it falls outside the frozen mechanism->function scope.

title: Modulation of Neuronal Interactions Through Neuronal Synchronization
rationale: OUT (strict CTC fork): empirical demonstration that interaction strength depends on gamma phase relation, but no PING/E-I loop named as the generator.

title: High-Frequency, Long-Range Coupling Between Prefrontal and Visual Cortex During Attention
rationale: OUT: iconic empirical CTC (FEF-V4 gamma coherence tracks attention) but it is phenomenology — it does not name PING/E-I as the generator that is the vehicle for the function.

title:
Fast-spiking, parvalbumin
            <sup>+</sup>
            GABAergic interneurons: From cellular design to microcircuit function
rationale: Jonas-lab Science review argues that the fast-signaling cellular design of PV interneurons is what generates gamma and thereby supports microcircuit functions (temporal coincidence detection, gain control, spike timing) — mechanism explicitly the vehicle for function.

title: Interhemispheric Synchronization of Oscillatory Neuronal Responses in Cat Visual Cortex
rationale: OUT: long-range synchronization phenomenology supporting binding; mechanism of gamma generation not the named vehicle.

title: Mechanisms of Gamma Oscillations
rationale: Canonical PING/ING mechanism review that also makes the functional argument that the E-I gamma cycle provides temporal windows for spike timing, coordination, and gain control.

title: Visual Feature Integration and the Temporal Correlation Hypothesis
rationale: OUT: the canonical binding-by-synchrony review, but its vehicle is synchrony-as-a-code generically; gamma generation by the E-I/PING loop is not the named mechanism the binding claim rests on.

title: Neuronal Gamma-Band Synchronization as a Fundamental Process in Cortical Computation
rationale: Annual Review that describes gamma as an inhibition-based (E-I) rhythm and argues the resulting duty cycle implements communication-through-coherence and gain; obvious hit but makes the mechanism->function argument.

title: Synchronization of Cortical Activity and Its Putative Role in Information Processing and Learning
rationale: Review tying network- (including inhibitory-interneuron) generated gamma synchronization to its putative functions in cortical information processing and learning.

title: Role of Interneuron Diversity in the Cortical Microcircuit for Attention
rationale: Shows distinct interneuron classes shaping the PING gamma loop implement attentional gain and routing, tying the E-I microcircuit to the attention function.

title: What Determines the Frequency of Fast Network Oscillations With Irregular  Neural Discharges? I. Synaptic Dynamics and Excitation-Inhibition  Balance
rationale: Analyses how synaptic dynamics and E-I balance set gamma frequency; purely a generating-mechanism study with no functional/cognitive claim attached.

title: Neurodynamics of Biased Competition and Cooperation for Attention: A Model With Spiking Neurons
rationale: A spiking biased-competition model, but the competition is driven by recurrent NMDA excitation and pooled inhibition at the rate level; gamma synchrony is a reported readout rather than the named vehicle for the competition, so it fails the PING-as-mechanism test.

title: Simulations of cortical pyramidal neurons synchronized by inhibitory interneurons
rationale: Explicitly makes reciprocal pyramidal-interneuron inhibition the generator of near-zero-phase synchrony and argues this E-I loop is the mechanism that phase-locks distributed pyramidal populations for assembly/binding.

title: Long-Range Synchronization of γ and β Oscillations and the Plasticity of Excitatory and Inhibitory Synapses: A Network Model
rationale: Network model in which the E-I gamma/beta mechanism plus synaptic plasticity produces zero-phase long-range synchrony, argued as the substrate for binding/communication across separated cortical sites.

title: Neurophysiological and Computational Principles of Cortical Rhythms in Cognition
rationale: Reviews PING/ING generation of cortical gamma and builds the mechanism-to-function argument that these E-I rhythms support attention, working memory, and inter-areal communication.

title: Synchronization in Networks of Excitatory and Inhibitory Neurons with Sparse, Random Connectivity
rationale: Foundational PING mechanism paper establishing when E-I networks synchronize and the suppression boundary, but it makes no cognitive/computational function claim — pure mechanism.

title: Rapid Temporal Modulation of Synchrony by Competition in Cortical Interneuron Networks
rationale: Competition among gamma-generating interneuron subnetworks rapidly reconfigures synchrony, providing a mechanism for fast attentional gating of which population is coherent.

title: Effects of Noisy Drive on Rhythms in Networks of Excitatory and Inhibitory Neurons
rationale: Analyzes how noise degrades PING/ING coherence and the suppression regime; substrate for later gating work but states no explicit cognitive function — mechanism only.

title: Stimulus Competition by Inhibitory Interference
rationale: Two stimuli entrain a shared interneuron network at different gamma phases and interfere, so inhibition-based gamma timing is the vehicle for stimulus competition/selection.

title: Synchronization of Bursting Action Potential Discharge in a Model Network of Neocortical Neurons
rationale: Reciprocal excitation-inhibition in the modeled cortical network is the named generator of synchronized (oscillatory) bursting, argued as the substrate for coherent cell-assembly signaling.

title: A Computer Simulation of Oscillatory Behavior in Primary Visual Cortex
rationale: Models how excitatory-inhibitory cortical circuitry produces oscillations (a proto-PING mechanism) but does not argue a specific cognitive function follows from it — pure mechanism.

title: Gamma Oscillations and Stimulus Selection
rationale: Shows that PING gamma sharpens which of several competing inputs drives the E-cells, making gamma the mechanism for stimulus selection/gain via the timing of interneuron feedback.

title: Selective Population Rate Coding: A Possible Computational Role of Gamma Oscillations in Selective Attention
rationale: OUT (borderline): argues gamma oscillations support selective population-rate coding for attention, but frames it as a coding scheme without invoking PING/E-I reciprocal interaction as the named generator of the rhythm.

title: Transforming Discoveries About Cortical Microcircuits and Gamma Oscillations Into New Treatments for Cognitive Deficits in Schizophrenia
rationale: review: PV-gamma microcircuit -> cognition (+therapeutics)

title: Gamma Oscillations of Spiking Neural Populations Enhance Signal Discrimination
rationale: E-I spiking network where population gamma sharpens the temporal window and enhances signal discrimination/coincidence readout — the E-I gamma rhythm is the vehicle for the discrimination gain.

title: Rhythm Generation through Period Concatenation in Rat Somatosensory Cortex
rationale: Mechanistic account of how beta arises by concatenating gamma/beta periods; no explicit cognitive-function argument tied to the E-I loop.

title: Optimal Information Transfer in the Cortex through Synchronization
rationale: Spiking E-I network showing that gamma synchronization between areas maximizes transfer entropy / information routing, giving a mechanistic account of communication-through-coherence — gamma from E-I interaction is the named vehicle.

title: Minimal Size of Cell Assemblies Coordinated by Gamma Oscillations
rationale: Derives the minimum assembly size PING gamma can lock, arguing gamma acts as a gating/coordination mechanism that only lets sufficiently large coincident cell assemblies participate — coincidence/assembly-gating function from E-I timing.

title: Dynamic Effective Connectivity of Inter-Areal Brain Circuits
rationale: Shows that the phase relations of E-I gamma oscillations between areas set the direction of effective connectivity, so PING-type rhythms are the vehicle for flexible information routing.

title: Efficient “Communication through Coherence” Requires Oscillations Structured to Minimize Interference between Signals
rationale: Analyzes how the structure of E-I gamma oscillations must be tuned to make communication-through-coherence work without cross-talk, making the rhythm the explicit vehicle for selective routing.

title: Top-Down Beta Rhythms Support Selective Attention via Interlaminar Interaction: A Model
rationale: Models selective attention as top-down beta modulating the bottom-up PING gamma across layers, making the E-I gamma loop the substrate that attention biases.

title: Phase-Coherence Transitions and Communication in the Gamma Range between Delay-Coupled Neuronal Populations
rationale: Gamma rhythms from delay-coupled E-I populations undergo phase-coherence transitions that gate the direction and efficacy of communication, the PING-range rhythm being the named vehicle.

title: Communication through Resonance in Spiking Neuronal Networks
rationale: Proposes communication-through-resonance in E-I spiking networks as an oscillation-based routing mechanism, an explicit alternative-to-CTC functional claim built on the E-I rhythm.

title: Input-Dependent Frequency Modulation of Cortical Gamma Oscillations Shapes Spatial Synchronization and Enables Phase Coding
rationale: Argues that input-driven frequency modulation of PING gamma organizes spatial synchronization and enables stimulus phase coding, so the E-I rhythm is the vehicle for the coding scheme.

title: Theta-Gamma Coding Meets Communication-through-Coherence: Neuronal Oscillatory Multiplexing Theories Reconciled
rationale: Reconciles phase-coding and CTC by modeling gamma (from an E-I inhibitory-window loop) as both a temporal reference for phase coding and the carrier for coherence-based selective communication.

title: Top-down control of cortical gamma-band communication via pulvinar induced phase shifts in the alpha rhythm
rationale: Model in which pulvinar-induced phase shifts of E-I gamma oscillators gate which cortical stream wins, making PING phase relations the explicit vehicle for top-down communication-through-coherence.

title: Flexible resonance in prefrontal networks with strong feedback inhibition
rationale: PING prefrontal model with strong feedback inhibition whose gamma resonance selectively amplifies inputs matching its rhythm, arguing input selection/gain routing follows from the E-I resonant mechanism.

title: Integration, coincidence detection and resonance in networks of spiking neurons expressing Gamma oscillations and asynchronous states
rationale: Networks in a gamma-oscillatory (E-I) regime are shown to switch cells from integrators to coincidence detectors/resonators, making the gamma-generating loop the explicit vehicle for the temporal-window computation.

title: Synaptic Mechanisms of Tight Spike Synchrony at Gamma Frequency in Cerebral Cortex
rationale: Out: a detailed PING/E-I mechanism paper for gamma spike synchrony with no cognitive/computational function claimed as the downstream consequence.

title: How Close Are We to Understanding What (if Anything) γ Oscillations Do in Cortical Circuits?
rationale: Skeptical review that evaluates whether PING/interneuron-generated gamma actually serves gain, routing and timing functions, engaging the mechanism-to-function argument critically.

title: Snapshots of the Brain in Action: Local Circuit Operations through the Lens of γ Oscillations
rationale: Reviews how the PV-interneuron PING loop generating gamma implements local gain control, gating and temporal windowing, arguing these computations follow from the E-I mechanism.

title: An Integrated Microcircuit Model of Attentional Processing in the Neocortex
rationale: An E-I microcircuit generating gamma via recurrent inhibition in which attention increases synchrony and firing rate to enhance the attended stimulus — the E-I gamma mechanism carries the attentional-gain function.

title: The highly irregular firing of cortical cells is inconsistent with temporal integration of random EPSPs
rationale: Argues cortical neurons act as coincidence detectors (the included coincidence/gain axis), but does not invoke a PING/E-I gamma loop as the generator of the coincident synchronous input.

title: Gamma Oscillation by Synaptic Inhibition in a Hippocampal Interneuronal Network Model
rationale: Foundational ING computational model; establishes the generation mechanism but does not argue for a downstream cognitive function.

title: Gamma-Phase Shifting in Awake Monkey Visual Cortex
rationale: Demonstrates that firing phase within the locally generated gamma cycle shifts with stimulus intensity and thereby carries stimulus information (phase-of-firing coding), with local E-I gamma as the carrier.

title: Impact of Correlated Synaptic Input on Output Firing Rate and Variability in Simple Neuronal Models
rationale: OUT: pure mechanism showing input correlations control output rate/variability (gain); no gamma rhythm and no PING/E-I generator invoked, so it fails the mechanism-names-PING requirement.

title: Rapid Signaling at Inhibitory Synapses in a Dentate Gyrus Interneuron Network
rationale: Synaptic physiology of the interneuron network underlying gamma; pure mechanism, no functional claim.

title: Oscillatory Neuronal Synchronization in Primary Visual Cortex as a Correlate of Stimulus Selection
rationale: Title itself frames gamma synchrony as a correlate of stimulus selection; no generating E-I/PING mechanism is invoked, so it is phenomenology, not a mechanism->function argument.

title: Oscillations, Phase-of-Firing Coding, and Spike Timing-Dependent Plasticity: An Efficient Learning Scheme
rationale: Out: the gamma oscillation is imposed as an external/LFP drive used as a temporal reference for phase-of-firing coding and STDP — the rhythm is assumed, not generated by a named PING/E-I loop, so the mechanism is not the vehicle.

title: Dual Gamma Rhythm Generators Control Interlaminar Synchrony in Auditory Cortex
rationale: Distinguishes a PING (synaptic E-I) gamma generator from a persistent ING/gap-junction generator and argues their interaction controls interlaminar synchrony, i.e. communication/routing between cortical layers follows from the E-I timing.

title: Synchrony Makes Neurons Fire in Sequence, and Stimulus Properties Determine Who Is Ahead
rationale: Shows stimulus drive determines relative firing phase within the gamma cycle (a phase code by stimulus strength), with the inhibition-based gamma rhythm as the temporal reference frame — the E-I gamma mechanism is the vehicle for the phase code.

title: Postnatal Differentiation of Basket Cells from Slow to Fast Signaling Devices
rationale: Developmental maturation of basket-cell signaling speed enabling gamma; pure mechanism, no functional claim.

title: Top-Down Beta Enhances Bottom-Up Gamma
rationale: Empirical+conceptual: top-down beta influence enhances feedforward gamma synchronization used for attentional routing (communication-through-coherence); gamma is the E-I-generated carrier — borderline on mechanism-naming but argues routing from the rhythm.

title: Reconciling Coherent Oscillation with Modulationof Irregular Spiking Activity in Selective Attention:Gamma-Range Synchronization between Sensoryand Executive Cortical Areas
rationale: A spiking E-I microcircuit generates gamma via recurrent inhibition and the paper argues this coherence between sensory and executive areas implements the firing-rate modulation of selective attention.

title: The Effects of Visual Stimulation and Selective Visual Attention on Rhythmic Neuronal Synchronization in Macaque Area V4
rationale: Empirical demonstration that attention modulates gamma synchronization — phenomenology with no PING/E-I generating mechanism argued as the vehicle for the effect.

title: The Neuronal Basis of Attention: Rate versus Synchronization Modulation
rationale: A spiking E-I network shows that attention is implemented via gamma synchronization modulation (not just rate), with the E-I-generated rhythm as the vehicle for the attentional effect.

title: A Second Function of Gamma Frequency Oscillations: An E%-Max Winner-Take-All Mechanism Selects Which Cells Fire
rationale: Feedback interneuron (E-I) gamma creates a within-cycle rising inhibition that acts as a winner-take-all selecting only the most strongly/coincidently driven cells—function derived directly from the gamma generating loop.

title: Establishing communication between neuronal populations through competitive entrainment
rationale: E-I oscillator populations compete to entrain a target's gamma, and the winner establishes a communication channel — competitive gamma entrainment is the vehicle for CTC-style selective routing.

title: Emergent bimodal firing patterns implement different encoding strategies during gamma-band oscillations
rationale: Out: about encoding/firing patterns during gamma rather than a routing/attention function that follows from the E-I gamma mechanism; functional-communication claim is not the vehicle.

title: Toggling between gamma-frequency activity and suppression of cell assemblies
rationale: Uses the PING E-I loop to show how small changes in drive toggle an assembly between gamma participation and inhibitory suppression, giving an attentional gate/switch for cell assemblies.

title: Phase Difference between Model Cortical Areas Determines Level of Information Transfer
rationale: Two coupled PING networks show information transfer is governed by their gamma phase difference, directly instantiating the E-I-rhythm-as-router claim of CTC.

title: What do We Gain from Gamma? Local Dynamic Gain Modulation Drives Enhanced Efficacy and Efficiency of Signal Transmission
rationale: Models PING gamma as a local dynamic gain mechanism, claiming the E-I rhythm's inhibitory window boosts efficacy/efficiency of signal transmission — gamma-from-PING is the named vehicle for gain control.

title: Are Different Rhythms Good for Different Functions?
rationale: Argues each E-I rhythm has a computational niche, with PING gamma assigned to local competition/attention and gating — explicit mechanism-to-function assignment.

title: Mechanisms for Phase Shifting in Cortical Networks and their Role in Communication through Coherence
rationale: Directly analyses how E-I (PING/ING) network parameters set the gamma phase, and argues phase-shifting of the inhibition-based rhythm is the mechanism enabling communication-through-coherence routing — strict-CTC in-scope.

title: More Gamma More Predictions: Gamma-Synchronization as a Key Mechanism for Efficient Integration of Classical Receptive Field Inputs with Surround Predictions
rationale: Proposes that receptive-field-generated PING gamma synchronization is the mechanism that makes surround/predictive inputs act as effective coincident drive, tying the E-I rhythm to predictive integration and gain.

title: A quantitative theory of gamma synchronization in macaque V1
rationale: Out: primarily a quantitative theory/phenomenology of gamma synchronization frequency and phase; it engages CTC but does not itself make a PING-mechanism->function claim as its central result.

title: A neural circuit for gamma-band coherence across the retinotopic map in mouse visual cortex
rationale: SST-based generator, not PV/PING (OUT)

title: Interneuron-specific gamma synchronization indexes cue uncertainty and prediction errors in lateral prefrontal and anterior cingulate cortex
rationale: Interneuron-to-pyramidal gamma synchronization (PING signature) tracks cue uncertainty and prediction errors, arguing the E-I gamma loop is the vehicle for encoding these decision variables.
